map.subtitle: Big Idea 1: Creative Development   •   2 days   •   LO CRD-2.A, CRD-2.B, CRD-2.C, CRD-2.D
map.intro: Everything for Topic 1.2 lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Days 1–2 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, cold-call prompts, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with QOTD warm-ups, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Days 1–2 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: Behavior Detective — The LunchDash Log and Exercise 2: Trigger Tracer (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — Purpose, Triggers, and Machines That Wait (3-part: Opening Day 1, Mid-lesson Day 2, Closing Day 2)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — 8 questions across LO CRD-2.A, CRD-2.B, CRD-2.C, and CRD-2.D, canonical in the Day-1 master JSON)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the daily instruction you project and teach from (Day 1: purpose of innovations, programs vs. code segments vs. behavior, the two description levels; Day 2: inputs, events, event-driven vs. sequential, and outputs, closing with Common AP Traps and the exit check).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.

Naming: the CED topic is 'Program Function and Purpose'; the running examples across both days are the mystery guessing app (Day 1 bell ringer, described only by its behavior) and the tap-that-jumped-the-queue phone puzzle (Day 1 teaser → Day 2 bell ringer). All materials refer to this one topic.

exercise1.title: Behavior Detective — The LunchDash Log
exercise1.intro: You are the new developer assigned to LunchDash, the school's (fictional) lunch pre-ordering app — and your onboarding task is to understand it purely from its behavior log. For each logged moment you will identify the inputs (source and form), the events that supplied them, and the outputs (form and destination device) — then describe the program's function at both CED description levels. Part A works the log table; Part B extends the same skills. Everything maps to CB Learning Objectives CRD-2.B, CRD-2.C, and CRD-2.D.
exercise1.scenarioTitle: LunchDash Behavior Log: Five Moments Under Review
exercise1.narrative: The lead developer hands you five entries pulled from yesterday's behavior log and says: 'Explain what the program is doing in each one, using the right vocabulary, and you're on the team.' Read the log row by row, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: anchor your first instinct, trace what data crossed the program's boundary in each direction, then name each piece with CED vocabulary — input or output, its form, its source or destination, and what triggered the code to run.

rows: A student taps the pizza tile on the touchscreen
An order confirmation appears on screen and the phone buzzes once
One tap produced two separate reactions in two different forms
The cafeteria's inventory software transmits 'pizza: 0 remaining'
The pizza tile turns gray and shows SOLD OUT
No human touched anything — the data arrived from other software
A student says 'Reorder my usual' to the app's voice assistant
The app queues the exact order that student placed the last three Fridays
The reply used data no microphone captured today
Nothing — no orders for 20 minutes
The app does nothing visible, then reacts instantly to the 11:00 tap
Twenty idle minutes, then an immediate response with no delay
Two students tap the same 'Surprise me' button seconds apart
One student is offered pizza; the other is offered the salad bowl
Identical taps on the identical button produced different offers

exercise1.scenarioNote: Rows adapted from the Day 1 mystery-app bell ringer and the Day 2 input/output worked table; all terms and EKs follow the College Board AP Computer Science Principles Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2023).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: which one row would most confuse someone who believes 'programs always run top to bottom, and the same input always gives the same output' — and why?
exercise1.partALabel: Part A - Rulings on the Log

partA.ek: EK CRD-2.C.1 / EK CRD-2.C.2 / EK CRD-2.D.1
partA.q: Row 1: name the input's form, the event that supplied it, and BOTH outputs with their forms. Use CED vocabulary for every piece.
partA.answer: The input is the tap — TACTILE input from a user (EK CRD-2.C.1 names tactile as an input form). The tap is an EVENT: it is associated with an action (touching the tile) and supplies input data to the program (EK CRD-2.C.2 — which tile was touched). The two outputs (EK CRD-2.D.1: any data sent from a program to a device) are the on-screen confirmation, VISUAL form sent to the display, and the buzz, TACTILE form sent to the vibration motor. Full credit requires all four labels: input form, the event, and both output forms — noting that one input can legitimately produce multiple outputs.

partA.q: Row 2: no person touched anything, yet the program clearly received input. Identify the input's source, cite the rule that makes this legal, and explain what would be wrong with saying 'the app had no input.'
partA.answer: The input source is ANOTHER PROGRAM — the cafeteria's inventory software. Per EK CRD-2.C.6, input can come from a user OR other programs, so software-to-software input is completely normal. Saying 'the app had no input' is wrong because data ('pizza: 0 remaining') was sent to the computer for processing by the program (EK CRD-2.C.1's definition of input) — the tile turning gray is output that the input clearly caused (inputs usually affect the output produced, EK CRD-2.C.4). Full credit names the other-program source and rejects the humans-only assumption explicitly.

partA.q: Row 4: a classmate says the app must have been 'stuck on line 200' during the idle 20 minutes, waiting for the next line to run. Rule on this using the CED's exact standard: is LunchDash sequential or event-driven, and what really 'happened' during the idle window?
partA.answer: LunchDash is EVENT-DRIVEN: per EK CRD-2.C.5, in event-driven programming, program statements are executed when TRIGGERED rather than through the sequential flow of control. The order-handling code has no fixed place 'in line' — it ran at 11:00 because the tap event fired (events are generated when a key is pressed, a mouse is clicked, or any other defined action occurs, EK CRD-2.C.3), not because the program finally reached line 200. During the idle window, no order-handling statements ran at all because no event triggered them; the 'instant response' after 20 quiet minutes is the signature of event-driven execution. Full credit: name the model, quote the triggered-vs-sequential distinction, and reject the 'stuck on a line' picture.

partA.q: Row 5: identical taps produced different offers, and a student files a bug report claiming the program is broken. Accept or reject the bug report, citing the rule — and name what the program must be remembering.
partA.answer: REJECT the bug report. Per EK CRD-2.D.2, program output is usually based on a program's input OR PRIOR STATE (e.g., internal values). The taps (inputs) were identical, but each student's prior state differed — the app is remembering internal values such as each student's order history, allergy settings, or past 'surprise' offers, and computing the offer from tap + remembered values. Different prior state legitimately yields different output from the same input; nothing here violates any rule the CED states. Full credit requires citing prior state / internal values and naming at least one plausible remembered value — 'the app is random' without the prior-state rule is not a full answer.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: You now understand LunchDash from its log alone. Describe the program at BOTH CED description levels: (a) one broad sentence saying what it does; (b) a 3–4 step detailed version that says what it does AND how its statements accomplish it.
partB.answer: Broad (what it does): 'LunchDash lets students pre-order cafeteria lunches from their phones.' Detailed (what + how, per EK CRD-2.B.5): (1) it waits for input events — taps, voice commands, or data from the cafeteria's inventory software; (2) when an event fires, it processes the input along with stored values like order history and item availability; (3) it updates its internal state (queued orders, remaining stock); and (4) it sends outputs to devices — confirmations and menus to the screen, buzzes to the vibration motor. Any accurate steps earn credit; the broad version must stay to WHAT it does with no mechanism, and the detailed version must include HOW.

partB.ek: EK CRD-2.B.1 / EK CRD-2.B.2 / EK CRD-2.B.4
partB.q: Classify each artifact with the precise CED term — program, code segment, or behavior: (a) the entire installed LunchDash app; (b) the 9 lines inside it that gray out a sold-out tile; (c) the sentence 'when a student taps a tile, a confirmation appears and the phone buzzes.'
partB.answer: (a) The entire installed app is a PROGRAM — a collection of program statements that performs a specific task when run by a computer, often referred to as software (EK CRD-2.B.1). (b) The 9 gray-out lines are a CODE SEGMENT — a collection of program statements that is PART of a program (EK CRD-2.B.2); they cannot stand alone. (c) The tap-confirmation sentence is BEHAVIOR — how the program functions during execution, described by how a user interacts with it (EK CRD-2.B.4). Full credit requires all three labels with the part-vs-whole and during-execution reasoning, not just the bare terms.

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: During row 4's idle window the app looked like it was running 'no code at all' — yet it answered the 11:00 tap instantly. Using the event-loop deep dive, explain what was actually running during those 20 minutes, and why a 'frozen' app is different from an idle one.
partB.answer: During the idle window the app's EVENT LOOP was running the whole time: a hidden cycle that repeatedly checks the event queue and dispatches each new event to its handler. No ORDER-HANDLING statements ran, because no events arrived — but the loop itself never stopped, which is why the 11:00 tap was answered instantly: the tap was stamped into the queue and dispatched on the loop's very next pass. A FROZEN app is the opposite failure: one handler is hogging the loop and never returning control, so events pile up in the queue unanswered. Idle = empty queue, spinning loop; frozen = full queue, blocked loop. Strong answers draw exactly that contrast.

exercise2.title: Trigger Tracer
exercise2.subtitle: 4 traces - LO CRD-2.C, CRD-2.D
exercise2.intro: You are the systems analyst on call. Four programs arrive with their behavior described — and your job is to trace the data. For each trace, identify every input (source: user or other program; form: tactile, audio, visual, or text) and every output (form and destination device), then rule on the execution model: sequential or event-driven, naming the trigger if there is one. Advanced students also layer the prior-state question: could this program answer the same input differently tomorrow, and what remembered value would explain it? Vague answers like 'the user gives input and gets output' score zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): every input named with source + form, every output named with form, and the execution model ruled sequential or event-driven with the trigger identified, for all 4 traces. Enrichment (optional): also state whether the output depends on prior state, naming the internal value the program must remember.
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every trace
method: Anchor your gut read before analyzing: what goes in, what comes out, and does anything here run 'when' something happens? Writing your own trace first keeps a convincing wrong label from swaying you.

Follow every piece of data across the program's edge. Inbound: name the source (user or other program) and the form (tactile, audio, visual, text). Outbound: name the form and the device it is sent to. One event can carry input; one input can trigger several outputs.

If statements ran because they were next in line, the flow is sequential. If they ran WHEN a defined action occurred — key press, click, program start, arriving data — name that event and rule event-driven. A ruling without the named trigger is an incomplete answer.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment (the prior-state layer): the strongest traces ask one more question — would the SAME input produce the SAME output tomorrow? If not, the program is consulting prior state: internal values it remembers, like a saved target temperature, a play history, or an account balance. Naming the remembered value turns 'it acted weird' into 'output is based on input or prior state,' which is exactly the sentence the exam rewards.
fields: Inputs and outputs traced (source, form, destination):
Execution model + trigger (sequential or event-driven, and why):

scenarios.text: At a crosswalk, a pedestrian presses the metal button. The controller program lights the WALK symbol and plays a chirping tone for pedestrians who can't see the signal. Between presses, the crosswalk program keeps running the normal traffic-light cycle.

key.signs: one inbound press, two outbound signals. The button press is TACTILE input from a USER (EK CRD-2.C.1); it is an event — a defined action that supplies input data to the program (EK CRD-2.C.2). The outputs are the WALK symbol, VISUAL form sent to the signal display, and the chirp, AUDIO form sent to the speaker (EK CRD-2.D.1: any data sent from a program to a device) — one input, two outputs in two forms.
key.mechanism: the walk-request handling is EVENT-DRIVEN — the WALK statements execute when the press event triggers them, not because they were next in line (EK CRD-2.C.5). The trigger is the button-press event. Note the hybrid: the background light cycle steps through its phases in order, but the pedestrian feature only ever runs when triggered, which is what the ruling must name.
key.subtype: prior state, minor but real: the controller remembers where it is in the light cycle, so an identical press can produce WALK immediately or after a delay depending on the cycle's current phase — same input, different timing of output, explained by an internal value (EK CRD-2.D.2).

scenarios.text: Every night at 2:00 a.m., the district gradebook software transmits the day's grades to the student portal program. The portal recalculates each student's average and emails a summary to families who turned on notifications. No person is awake, and nothing appears on any screen.

key.signs: input with zero humans involved. The grade data is input from ANOTHER PROGRAM — the gradebook software (EK CRD-2.C.6: input can come from a user or other programs) — arriving as TEXT/data form. The output is the emailed summary, TEXT form sent to mail servers and then to families' devices (EK CRD-2.D.1). 'Nothing appeared on a screen' does not mean no output: output is any data sent to a device, and email leaving the program qualifies.
key.mechanism: event-driven — the portal's recalculation statements execute when triggered by a defined action: the 2:00 a.m. arrival of the gradebook's transmission (EK CRD-2.C.3 allows 'any other defined action' as an event source, and EK CRD-2.C.5 supplies the ruling language). The portal was not grinding through lines all night waiting to 'reach' the email code; the trigger fired and the handler ran. The defense must name the arriving data (or the scheduled start) as the event.
key.subtype: prior state on two levels: the recalculated average depends on all PREVIOUSLY stored grades, not just tonight's input, and whether a family gets the email at all depends on a remembered notification setting — identical incoming grades produce different outputs per family because internal values differ (EK CRD-2.D.2).

scenarios.text: A smart doorbell detects motion on the porch. Its program records a 10-second clip, sends a notification to the homeowner's phone, and — only when the household set 'night mode' earlier that evening — also switches on the porch spotlight. The same raccoon at 2 p.m. gets no spotlight.

key.signs: the camera's motion detection is VISUAL input (EK CRD-2.C.1) and the motion event is the defined action supplying input data (EK CRD-2.C.2–C.3). Outputs, per EK CRD-2.D.1: the recorded clip, visual data sent to storage; the notification, text/visual form sent to the phone; and conditionally the spotlight command, sent to the light — three outputs from one event, one of them conditional.
key.mechanism: event-driven, with the motion event as the trigger: the record-notify-light statements execute when triggered rather than through sequential flow (EK CRD-2.C.5). The defense must also explain the raccoon: identical motion input at 2 p.m. and 2 a.m. produced different outputs because output is based on input AND prior state — the 'night mode' value the household stored earlier (EK CRD-2.D.2). The spotlight difference is not a second kind of input; it is remembered state consulted at trigger time.
key.subtype: the night-mode flag is the textbook internal value: set hours before the event, invisible in the moment, and decisive for the output. Strong answers generalize the pattern — any 'only when a setting is on' behavior means the program is combining the live event with stored state, so testing it demands varying BOTH the input and the state.

scenarios.text: In a rhythm game, a streaming server delivers each week's new song data to the game. During play, the player taps falling notes on the touchscreen; hits make the phone flash the note, buzz briefly, and raise a combo multiplier that makes later notes worth more. Two players tapping identical patterns can end with different scores if one started the song with a 'streak bonus' from yesterday.

key.signs: two distinct input streams: the song data from the streaming server — input from ANOTHER PROGRAM in text/data form (EK CRD-2.C.6) — and the player's taps, TACTILE input from a USER, each tap an event supplying which note was hit and when (EK CRD-2.C.1–C.3). Outputs in three forms: visual (the flash), tactile (the buzz), and the on-screen score, all data sent from the program to devices (EK CRD-2.D.1).
key.mechanism: event-driven through and through — tap handlers execute when triggered, not in a fixed line order, and the weekly song delivery is itself an event from another program (EK CRD-2.C.5). The scoring rule is the prior-state showcase: each hit's value depends on the input (the tap) AND internal values (the current combo multiplier, yesterday's streak bonus), so identical tap patterns legally produce different scores (EK CRD-2.D.2). The full defense traces both input sources, all three output forms, the trigger, and the remembered values — anything less is a partial trace.
key.subtype: layered state: the combo multiplier is state that the program itself wrote earlier in the SAME run, while the streak bonus is state persisted from a PREVIOUS run — same rule (output depends on prior state), two different lifetimes. Distinguishing within-run state from saved-across-runs state is the mark of a complete challenge-level answer.

discussion.title: Purpose, Triggers, and Machines That Wait
discussion.minutes: ~25 min total - 3 parts - Opening (Day 1) + Mid-lesson (Day 2) + Closing (Day 2)
discussion.overview: Every app on your phone exists for one of two official reasons, reacts to you through events it was built to expect, and quietly remembers things between your taps. Across three short windows in this topic, your group will argue about whether 'fun' counts as a purpose, test how far a program can be understood from its behavior alone, and decide how comfortable you are with programs whose outputs depend on everything they remember about you.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. Pick the app your group would miss most. Is its purpose solving a problem, pursuing interests through creative expression — or both? Make the case, then test it: would the developers agree with your answer, and does it change what they should build next?

Question 2. In the bell ringer you described a program you never saw the code for. How far could that go — could you fully understand a program from its behavior alone? Name one thing behavior can tell you and one thing it can't.
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 2, ~8 min, right after the sequential vs. event-driven comparison
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - The trigger inventory
items.text: List every event your group's phones responded to in the last hour — taps, notifications, alarms, messages arriving. Then rule as a group: which of those events came from a USER, and which came from OTHER PROGRAMS? Write the one event whose source surprised you most and why.

items.label: Task B - Design the flow
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: your group must build a fire-alarm system for the school. Should its program be sequential or event-driven? Record your ruling, the trigger(s) it must respond to, and the strongest argument someone could make for the model you rejected.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 2, ~9 min, after the outputs and prior-state section
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The 'it knows me too well' case
items.text: A music app answers the identical request — 'play something I'd like' — differently for every person, every day, because its output is based on input AND prior state: everything it remembers about you. Build your group's ruling: where is the line between prior state that makes a program useful (your thermostat's target, your saved game) and prior state that starts to feel like surveillance? Name one remembered value your group would gladly let a program keep, and one you'd want it to forget.
Our group's verdict (the line we drew + one-sentence justification):
